title: The Dreamer
content:
Even as a child he loved to thrid the bowers,
    And mark the loafing sunlight's lazy laugh;
    Or, on each season, spell the epitaph
    Of its dead months repeated in their flowers;
    Or list the music of the strolling showers,
    Whose vagabond notes strummed through a twinkling staff,
    Or read the day's delivered monograph
    Through all the chapters of its d�dal hours.
    Still with the same child-faith and child regard
    He looks on Nature, hearing at her heart,
    The Beautiful beat out the time and place,
    Through which no lesson of this life is hard,
    No struggle vain of science or of art,
    That dies with failure written on its face.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Madison Julius Cawein represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Madison Julius Cawein's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
